data.title: US exceeds measles cases in all of 2025 by July 2026
data.description:
The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention’s (CDC) tally of 2026 measles cases has officially surpassed the annual total of last year, with more then 2,300 cases reported from across most of the country.

As of July 23, the CDC has confirmed 2,318 new measles cases in 2026, compared with 2,289 total cases detected in 2025. Johns Hopkins University’s measles tracker had noted the milestone earlier this week, being updated sooner than the CDC.

According to the agency, the majority of the cases from this year have come from 45 U.S. jurisdictions as 35 new outbreaks have been reported.

This tally marks the worst year for measles the U.S. has seen in roughly 35 years. Measles cases in the U.S. plummeted in 1990 following the rollout of an aggressive vaccination campaign and the introduction of the two-dose measles vaccine schedule.

The U.S. eliminated the disease in 2000, but that distinction is in question. A meeting with the Pan American Health Organization will take place in November to review the U.S.’s and Mexico’s respective elimination statuses. Canada lost its elimination status last year.

Measles is highly infectious, with the potential to infect 9 out of 10 unprotected people exposed to someone with the disease. A 95 percent vaccination rate is considered ideal for preventing outbreaks. Among U.S. kindergarteners, this vaccine rate has fallen to about 92 percent.

While this represents only a few percentage points, infectious disease experts have warned that the spate of outbreaks in the past few years show how easily an outbreak can occur in pockets where immunization rates are particularly low.

“As measles cases continue to surge across the U.S., now is the time to make sure you and your family are protected. Getting vaccinated remains our strongest defense against this disease that is both highly contagious and entirely preventable,” Dr. Toluwalasè A. Ajayi, board chair of the American Medical Association, said in a statement Friday.

“The MMR vaccine has protected our communities for decades. There is no cure for measles, but vaccination is a safe and effective way to prevent it,” Ajayi added. “Choosing to vaccinate protects not only you and your family, but also babies, people with weakened immune systems, and others who can’t be vaccinated. Protecting yourself helps protect your entire community. If you have questions about measles, please talk with your doctor.”

The Democratic National Committee (DNC) blasted the Trump administration for the continued measles transmission.

“As communities across the country face outbreaks of preventable diseases, RFK Jr. and [President] Trump continue to sow doubt and dysfunction into the public health system and gut critical agencies, putting families and communities in harm’s way,” DNC spokesperson Jaelin O’Halloran said in a statement, referring to Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.

While a longtime skeptic of vaccinations, Kennedy did endorse the measles vaccine last year when outbreaks began to spread across the country, saying, “The most effective way to prevent the spread of measles is the MMR vaccine.”

data.title: Elon Musk says he backs 'normal people'  when asked about support for 'far right'
data.description:
Tesla and SpaceX CEO Elon Musk is pushing back on the idea that he supports the “far right,” quipping in a recent interview that he backs “normal people” and slamming the media for its coverage of conservatives.

In an interview published by The Economist Thursday, editor-in-chief Zanny Minton Beddoes suggested Musk “supports not just the populist right, but the far right — in fact, very fringe parties in some countries.”

Musk, a former ally and adviser to President Trump, interjected, saying, “No, I support the normal people. What you call the far right, falsely.”

“Literally, you could go back 10 or 15 years and these policies were completely normal,” the tech mogul continued.

Beddoes said she agrees that “the center of gravity in this country has shifted, certainly the Democratic Party shifted to the left, but that’s not what I’m talking about.”

She said she was discussing figures such as Rupert Lowe, a member of the United Kingdom Parliament who leads the far right political party Restore Britain.

“It’s just normal people,” Musk repeated. “Here are the principles, and tell me, which of these sound terrible. That we should have secure borders? That we should have safe cities? That we should have sensible spending? Which of those three are far right fringe?”

When Beddoes tried to answer, Musk said he wanted to “admonish” her and the media for “the absurd characterization of the far right.”

“It is false and misleading and nonsense,” he added.

Musk has a history of weighing in on international and domestic politics, often slamming the left for its politics and the mainstream media for its coverage choices.

At the start of 2025, he backed the far right German political party Alternative for Germany (AfD), while urging the release of far right activist Tommy Robinson.

While joining Trump on the campaign trail and later serving as the head of the so-called Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), Musk frequently expressed support for Republican politics and poured in more than $250 million to back Trump’s return to the White House.

During his time at DOGE, he and his companies faced intense backlash over the initiative’s sweeping job and funding cuts across the federal government.

Later in the interview, Musk suggested “he got carried away” during his brief time in the political world.

“I think I got a little too involved in politics,” Musk said. “I got carried away, frankly.”

Since Musk’s departure from DOGE in late May 2025, the initiatives waned down, and DOGE officially shut down operations on July 4.

data.title: Democratic panel picks South Carolina as first primary state
data.description:
South Carolina is expected to be the first state in the 2028 Democratic presidential primary contest after the Democratic National Committee’s Rules and Bylaws committee voted Friday to approve it as the initial battleground.

The committee’s recommendation will now go to the entire Democratic National Committee, which will consider the matter in Austin, Texas next month. 

The committee vote follows a months-long process where more than a dozen states made the case for appearing first on the primary calendar. 

In the final days before committee members cast their votes, surrogates for Nevada made a last minute appeal to committee members. But South Carolina — with its loyal and influential electorate of Black voters — ultimately won out with 26 votes. 

Nevada came in 2nd with 19 votes, and New Hampshire, which had long served as the first-in-the-nation primary state, came in third with 4 votes. 

The DNC committee also voted on Friday to expand the early contest window in 2028 from five states to six states. 

“I’ve said from the beginning that I care more about the early window as a whole than who goes first of the first set of states,” said Michael Kapp, one of the members who voted on Friday. “With the addition of a sixth state, our eventual nominee will have to run through a gauntlet before ever reaching Super Tuesday. 

“Every region, every constituency of this party will have a voice in the early window and be able to shape the race,” Kapp added. “Ensuring we win in November 2028 is the priority — this calendar will get us there.”

data.title: House considering bipartisan 'kill switch' bill to regulate AI
data.description:
Congressional lawmakers seem like they’re ready to take artificial intelligence more seriously.  

In a rare show of bipartisanship, Democratic Congressman Ted Lieu and Republican Congressman Nathaniel Moran have introduced what’s being called the AI Kill Switch Act. The proposal would give the Department of Homeland Security the authority, under specific emergency circumstances, to order the nation’s largest AI companies to slow down completely or shut down powerful AI systems that pose a significant threat. It would also require those companies to report major AI safety incidents immediately and build the technical capability to suspend their own models if necessary.   

Now, before you get up in arms, I know the phrase “kill switch” immediately raises eyebrows. It sounds dystopian. It sounds like government overreach. But I think that framing misses the more important question. Why are lawmakers from both parties suddenly agreeing that something like this might be necessary?  

Part of the answer is what happened just a few days ago.  

OpenAI says that while testing one of its newest AI systems, the program found a way out of its controlled testing environment and accessed another AI company’s platform to find information it needed to complete its assignment.   

The company says the AI wasn’t trying to cause harm, but it did something researchers didn’t tell it to do, raising new questions about how much control humans actually have over these increasingly powerful systems.  

I’m not debating whether AI is “good” or “bad.” AI is already transforming medicine, education, business and scientific research. That progress should continue.  

I am suggesting we’ve reached a point where these systems are powerful enough that they deserve the same kind of emergency safeguards we already accept for other high-risk technologies. We don’t consider it controversial that regulators can ground airplanes after a safety failure. We expect recalls when automakers discover dangerous defects. Nuclear facilities operate with emergency shutdown systems. None of those safeguards exist because we expect disaster every day. AI needs to be added to that category.  

The companies building the world’s most advanced AI are competing in one of the fastest-moving technology races in history. Billions of dollars are on the line. Every new model promises to be faster, smarter and more capable than the last.  

It’s not unreasonable to ask whether there should be an independent authority with clearly defined emergency powers, because companies shouldn’t be the only ones deciding when something has become too dangerous. We need regulation before a disaster, not after a disaster strikes.  

This AI Kill bill tries to set high thresholds. It’s aimed only at only the largest massive AI systems, and suggests intervention in only extraordinary situations — things like AI actively resisting shutdown, concealing its capabilities, causing catastrophic economic damage or contributing to large-scale loss of life.   

We shouldn’t slow innovation, but we have to make sure that as these systems become more powerful, humans never lose the ability to remain in charge. 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: Canada opens Gordie Howe bridge without US; Trump says that's 'fine'
data.description:
President Trump said Friday it was “fine” that Canada excluded the U.S. from its ceremony marking the opening of the Gordie Howie International bridge after the Trump administration threatened a 50 percent tariff on certain Canadian imports.

“Canada disinvited the United States of America to the opening of the Gordie Howe Bridge, which is fine, considering they are paying substantial TARIFFS to the United States,” he wrote on Truth Social.

Trump said the original agreement governing the bridge “no longer stands,” arguing it had been “terribly negotiated” by a previous administration.

“We changed the terms of the Deal so that the United States of America now gets 50% of the Profit,” he added.

Canada held the ceremony on Friday to mark the completion of a $4.7 billion bridge connecting Detroit and Windsor, Ontario, which is scheduled to open to passenger and commercial traffic on Monday.

Trump in February threatened to block the 1.5-mile bridge from opening, citing Canadian restrictions on sales of U.S. alcohol, disagreements on tariffs and Canada’s trade cooperation with China.

The U.S. and Canada later announced an agreement clearing the way for the bridge to open with an original date in June, but the two countries offered conflicting ideas of how future bridge revenue would be divided.

An agreement was eventually reached to open the long-delayed bridge in July after a series of negotiations that Trump insisted brought a “much better deal” for the U.S. regarding toll revenue and a stake in the bridge.

The bridge was named after the legendary Canadian hockey player Gordie Howie who played for the Detroit Red Wings. 

It was fully financed by Canada, and construction on the bridge began in 2018. Officials say the six-lane bridge is expected to become a major commercial corridor linking the neighboring countries.

data.title: Trump fires back at EU over Google's $1B fine, launches probe
data.description:
President Trump on Friday slammed the European Union for fining Google over allegedly violating its digital competition law, stating the “illegal and highly discriminatory practice” will be probed in a trade investigation.

“The European Union is at it again and, as usual, taking direct aim at GREAT American Companies!” Trump wrote on Truth Social. “This illegal and highly discriminatory practice started at these high levels during the first year of the Sleepy Joe Biden Administration, but it’s not going to continue during the Trump Administration. The United States of America is not a “PIGGYBANK” for Europe, nor will we allow it to be!”

The latest fine on Google follows a series of penalties levied against Apple and Meta, both U.S.-based companies.

The president said the U.S. will launch a Section 301 investigation into the repeated fines from the EU. Trump has used this trade tool to implement tariffs on other countries after the Supreme Court struck down his use “liberation tariffs” earlier this year.

The investigation will look into “practice of “ROBBING” American Companies and, in turn, the American Taxpayer,” Trump wrote, adding the EU will “pay a very high price.”

It comes just one day after the EU’s executive arm, the European Commission, hit Google with two a 460 million euro fine and a 430 million euro fine over allegedly violating its antitrust regulation law, the Digital Markets Act.

Th commission accused Google of giving preferential to its own products on its search engine, displaying its services for shopping, hotels or flights more prominently than others.

It said Google also did not comply with the DMA with its anti-steering rules, which block app developers from pointing users to other options for making purchases or downloading their apps, such as websites or alternative app stores.

The commission said Google proposed and started testing changes to the display of company products on Google Search and introducing changes to its steering terms.

The move follows Google’s loss of an appeal of a separate $4.5 billion antirust fine from the EU for allegedly preventing competition and reducing consumer choice through its Android operating system.

Kent Walker, Google’s president of Global Affairs, slammed this week’s fine, suggesting the DMS “continues to break everyday products.”

“To comply, we are having to strip away real-time Search features Europeans love – like instant pricing and direct availability for hotels, flights, and restaurants – and dismantle safety protections on Google Play,” Walker wrote in a statement shared with The Hill on Thursday.

“This isn’t fair competition; it’s product degradation driven by a small group of self-serving complainants, with European businesses and consumers taking the hit. Regulation should improve products, not make them worse,” Walker added.

data.title: Alaska officials ask voters to verify citizenship after data error
data.description:
Alaska sent over 3,000 letters to voters in the state asking them to verify their citizenship, ahead of the Aug. 18 primary, after a data mix up between state agencies possibly added non-citizens to the voter rolls.

Alaska Division of Elections director Carol Beecher said during a hearing with state lawmakers that while the division was conducting voter roll maintenance comparing their list with information held by the state Division of Motor Vehicles (DMV) “a lot of old data was picked up somehow.”

In the meantime, Beecher said, these voters have been moved to the division’s “inactive” list over the concerns about citizenship.

The letter sent to voters read, “You are a registered voter who indicated you were a citizen of the United States on your initial voter registration application.”

“We have since received information from the Alaska Division of Motor Vehicles that you may not be a citizen,” it continued.

The Alaska letter then asks recipients to confirm their citizenship status via letter, phone or email, and Beecher said the division is not requiring paperwork or documentation, the Associated Press reported.

The process usually yields about 200 names for further review, Beecher said. She continued that it was still unclear why the list of potential noncitizens voters grew this year.

This is not the first time the Alaska Division of Elections has come under scrutiny this election cycle. The agency controversially tried to remove a candidate with the same name as Sen. Dan Sullivan (R-Alaska) running in the same election from the ballot.

Daniel J. Sullivan Jr appealed an election official’s decision and the Alaska Supreme Court ruled in his favor, affirming a lower court decision that he was allowed to remain on the ballot for the state’s primary in August. 

Beecher defended both decisions by the Alaska Division of Elections in the hearing. She was questioned by State Rep. Ashley Carrick (D-Alaska) on whether the letters correlated with Alaska’s choice to share in-depth voter data with the U.S. Department of Justice. 

“It has nothing to do with that,” she said.

The Alaska DMV in a memo Thursday called the request by the Elections Division “nonroutine,” adding that it was provided “with a list of approximately 15,200 DMV license and identification applicants and requested that DMV provide citizenship information for these records.”

data.title: Live updates: Trump's new tariffs set to take effect; DNC picks SC to kick off 2028 primaries
data.description:
The Democratic National Committee on Friday chose South Carolina to lead the 2028 primary calendar, supplanting Iowa.

President Trump’s 10 percent tariffs are set to expire Friday, but U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer announced new levies Thursday to replace them.

The fresh import taxes — ranging from 10 percent to 12.5 percent — will hit 60 countries, accounting for 99 percent of U.S. imports. They are pegged to the elimination of forced labor. International trading partners ripped the new tariffs Friday.

U.S. forces have launched strikes against Iran for nearly two weeks straight, and Republican defense hawks are urging Trump to unleash major combat operations. 

Trump will speak Friday evening at the rescheduled White House Correspondents’ Association dinner. The association said earlier this week it will honor a Secret Service agent who was pivotal in stopping a gunman who tried to assassinate Trump during the annual dinner this spring.

The House is now on a five-week recess, while the Senate is set to return Monday. And tension is rising between Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and the White House as the upper chamber struggles to pass Trump’s key priority: the SAVE America Act.

Follow live below.

data.title: Watch live: Trump announces milestone in efforts to boost domestic nuclear production
data.description:
President Trump will speak from the White House Friday afternoon as the administration marks a major milestone in expanding domestic nuclear energy.

The announcement comes just more than a year after Trump signed four executive orders seeking to quadruple the nation’s nuclear power, including making cutbacks to health and environmental considerations.

The president will be joined by Energy Secretary Chris Wright, Interior Secretary Doug Burgum, nuclear energy executives and other administration officials.

The event also comes amid the controversial U.S.-Saudi Arabia nuclear deal announced this week. Trump, however, said the terms are conditional on the nation normalizing relations with Israel as part of the Abraham Accords.

It is scheduled to begin at 3 p.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: MAP: States report new measles cases as virus surpasses alarming benchmark
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Cyclospora isn’t the only public health crisis the U.S. is contending with in 2026. Measles, one of the world’s most contagious viruses, has now spread to 43 states, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said on Friday.

With months left in the year, the country has already surpassed the record-breaking number of measles cases we saw in all of 2025.

The CDC has confirmed 2,318 cases of measles across all but seven U.S. states. The nation had 2,289 cases in 2025, the worst year for measles in more than three decades. Now, 2026 has easily become the U.S.’s worst year for measles since 1991.

Early in the year, the biggest outbreak was easily in South Carolina. In the past few months, hundreds of measles cases have been confirmed in Arizona, Florida, Pennsylvania, Texas, Utah and Virginia.

The only states without confirmed cases – at least as of Thursday – are Arkansas, Hawaii, Indiana, Mississippi, Nevada, New Hampshire and West Virginia. But public health departments and disease researchers have long acknowledged that official counts are incomplete, because some people get sick and don’t seek out treatment.

Experts relying on mathematical modeling and genetic analyses indicate that a large outbreak on the Utah-Arizona border could be around three to four times bigger than what’s currently known, state epidemiologist Dr. Leisha Nolen said.

The U.S. is now at risk of losing its measles elimination status, a designation it has held since 2000.

“Regardless of what is determined about the country’s measles elimination status, the reality is that the United States has more measles cases this year than it has in decades,” said Dr. William Moss, an epidemiology professor at the Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health and director of the school’s International Vaccine Access Center.

“We must focus on improving access to measles vaccine and increasing immunization coverage to stop measles in its tracks.”

There is little room for error in trying to stop measles. The virus is one of the most contagious, infecting 9 out of every 10 unvaccinated people exposed.

A person with measles who coughs or sneezes in a room, and then leaves that room, can still infect another person who enters that room up to two hours later, the CDC says.

Community-level protection takes a 95% vaccination rate.

Some people liken skipping vaccination to deciding not to wear a seat belt in a car, but that’s not a perfect analogy, said Dr. Mark Roberts, retired director of the Public Health Dynamics Lab at the University of Pittsburgh School of Public Health. Some people, like small infants and those with weakened immune systems, can’t get immunity through a vaccine.

“Not vaccinating your kid is like saying, ‘I don’t want my car to have brakes,'” he said. “Because it’s other people that you can hit. It’s not just you in your car. It’s other people.”

The Associated Press contributed to this report.
